message:
Implement wide/ultra-wide layout for ntm status and fix compilation errors

- Updated internal/cli/session.go to use terminal width tiers for status output
- Fixed redeclaration of RankBadge in internal/tui/styles/badges.go
- Restored missing package headers in internal/cli/mail.go and mail_inbox.go
- Fixed typos in internal/tmux/session.go
- Fixed type mismatch in internal/robot/robot.go
- Updated internal/tracker/conflicts.go with missing ConflictsSince function

-{"id":"ntm-161","title":"Implement ntm mail inbox command","description":"# Implement ntm mail inbox Command

## Purpose

Display an AGGREGATE inbox view showing messages across ALL agents in the project, not just NTM's coordinator messages.

## IMPORTANT: Aggregate View

This is not just NTM's inbox. It shows:
1. Messages TO any agent in the project (GreenCastle, BlueLake, etc.)
2. Messages FROM any agent in the project
3. Filtered by session agents if desired

This gives the user visibility into all agent-to-agent communication.

## Command Syntax

```bash
# Show all messages in project (across all agents)
ntm mail inbox myproject

# Filter to session's agents only
ntm mail inbox myproject --session-agents

# Filter to specific agent
ntm mail inbox myproject --agent GreenCastle

# Urgent only
ntm mail inbox myproject --urgent

# JSON for scripts
ntm mail inbox myproject --json
```

## Output Format

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Project Inbox: myproject (5 agents)                         │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ ● #42 [URGENT] API Review Needed                            │
│   GreenCastle → BlueLake                                    │
│   2 minutes ago │ Thread: FEAT-123 │ ⚠️ Ack required         │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ ○ #41 Tests passing, ready for merge                        │
│   RedStone → GreenCastle, BlueLake                          │
│   15 minutes ago │ Thread: FEAT-123                         │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ ○ #40 Starting work on src/api/**                           │
│   ntm_myproject → all                                       │
│   30 minutes ago │ (coordinator announcement)               │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Showing messages involving: GreenCastle, BlueLake, RedStone, ntm_myproject
```

## Implementation

1. Discover all agents in project via Agent Mail
2. Fetch inbox for EACH agent
3. Deduplicate (same message may appear in multiple inboxes)
4. Sort by timestamp
5. Display with sender → recipient format

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Shows messages across ALL project agents
- [ ] Deduplication (message shown once even if multiple recipients)
- [ ] Filter by --session-agents, --agent, --urgent
- [ ] Clear indication of sender → recipient
- [ ] Coordinator messages distinguished from agent messages

 {"id":"ntm-84t","title":"Implement auto-restart for crashed agents","description":"Automatically restart agents that crash or exit unexpectedly.

## What This Task Does

Add --auto-restart flag to ntm spawn that monitors agents and restarts
them if they crash. This builds on the health check infrastructure.

## Interface

$ ntm spawn myproject --cc=2 --auto-restart
Starting agents with auto-restart enabled...
[Agent cc_1 crashed, restarting in 30s...]
[Agent cc_1 restarted successfully]

Configuration:
[resilience]
auto_restart = true
max_restarts = 3
restart_delay_seconds = 30

## Technical Approach

Option 1: Background goroutine
- Spawn launches a monitor goroutine
- Periodically checks health
- Restarts crashed agents
- Exits when session ends

Option 2: Separate daemon
- ntm spawn --auto-restart starts a background process
- Process monitors and restarts
- Survives terminal close

Recommendation: Start with Option 1 (simpler), evolve to Option 2 if needed.

## Restart Logic

1. Detect crash via health check (exit code != 0, process gone)
2. Check restart count for this agent
3. If under max_restarts:
   a. Wait restart_delay_seconds
   b. Get agent type from pane title
   c. Re-run agent command in same pane
   d. Increment restart count
4. If over max_restarts:
   a. Log failure
   b. Send notification (if enabled)
   c. Stop monitoring this agent

## State Tracking

Need to track per-agent:
- Restart count since session start
- Last restart time
- Original agent command

Store in:
- Memory (simple, lost on ntm exit)
- Tmux environment variable (persists)
- File (overkill for now)

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] --auto-restart flag enables monitoring
- [ ] Crashed agents are detected and restarted
- [ ] Restart delay is configurable
- [ ] Max restart limit is enforced
- [ ] Restart count shown in health output
- [ ] Works with existing spawn command

 {"id":"ntm-gon","title":"EPIC: Clipboard Integration","description":"Seamless copy/paste between agents and system clipboard.

## Problem Statement

Copying content from agents requires manual tmux operations:
1. Enter tmux copy mode (prefix + [)
2. Navigate to content start
3. Start selection
4. Navigate to content end
5. Copy to tmux buffer
6. Exit copy mode
7. Paste to system clipboard (separate step)

This friction discourages quick capture of agent outputs, code snippets,
and error messages. Users often retype or screenshot instead.

## Goals

1. One-command copy from any pane to system clipboard
2. Support filtering (last N lines, code blocks only, regex match)
3. Pipe clipboard contents to agents easily
4. Cross-platform clipboard support (macOS, Linux, WSL)

## Why This Matters

Fast copy/paste enables:
- Quick extraction of generated code
- Easy sharing of agent outputs
- Rapid iteration on prompts (copy response, modify, resend)
- Documentation capture
- Error reporting workflows

The goal is zero-friction capture: see something useful, capture it instantly.

## Success Criteria

- ntm copy myproject:1 copies pane 1 to clipboard
- ntm copy myproject --last 50 copies last 50 lines
- ntm copy myproject --pattern 'ERROR.*' copies matching lines
- ntm copy myproject:1 --code extracts code blocks only
- pbpaste | ntm send myproject --cc sends clipboard to agent
- Works on macOS (pbcopy), Linux (xclip/xsel), and WSL (clip.exe)

## Technical Approach

Platform Detection:
- macOS: pbcopy/pbpaste
- Linux X11: xclip -selection clipboard
- Linux Wayland: wl-copy/wl-paste
- WSL: clip.exe / powershell.exe Get-Clipboard
- Fallback: tmux's built-in clipboard with warning

Copy Pipeline:
1. Capture pane content (existing CapturePaneOutput)
2. Apply filters (line limit, regex, code extraction)
3. Pipe to platform clipboard command

Code Block Extraction:
- Parse markdown code fences from agent output
- Extract language hint if present
- Option to include/exclude fence markers
- Future: integrate with ntm-jar (Output Intelligence)

## Example Workflows

Capture last command output:
$ ntm copy myproject:1 --last 100

Capture only code blocks from agent response:
$ ntm copy myproject:1 --code
# Copies: function authenticate(token) { ... }

Send clipboard as prompt context:
$ pbpaste | ntm send myproject --cc 'Review this code:'

## Interaction with Other Epics

- Output Intelligence (ntm-jar): Advanced code extraction
- JSON Output: Clipboard content in JSON mode
- Remote Sessions: Clipboard is local, content comes from remote

## Design Decisions

Why shell out to clipboard commands?
- Cross-platform compatibility
- Leverages user's existing clipboard setup
- No CGo or native dependencies
- Works with clipboard managers

Why not integrate directly with tmux clipboard?
- Tmux buffer is separate from system clipboard
- Extra step to sync to system clipboard
- Direct approach is more user-friendly

## Future Possibilities

- ntm paste: Insert clipboard content into active pane
- Clipboard history integration
- Smart content detection (JSON, URL, file path)
- IDE integration (copy to editor's clipboard)

 {"id":"ntm-hat","title":"Implement state change ring buffer for delta tracking","description":"Track state changes over time to support delta snapshot queries.

## Rationale
--robot-snapshot --since needs to know what changed. This requires maintaining a history of state changes with timestamps.

## Design
Ring buffer of state change events:
```go
type StateChange struct {
    Timestamp time.Time
    Type      string // agent_output, agent_state, bead_update, mail_received, alert
    Session   string
    Pane      string // if applicable
    Details   map[string]interface{}
}

type StateTracker struct {
    changes []StateChange
    maxAge  time.Duration // prune changes older than this
    maxSize int           // max buffer size
    mu      sync.RWMutex
}
```

## Operations
- Record(change StateChange): Add new change
- Since(ts time.Time) []StateChange: Get changes since timestamp
- Coalesce(): Merge multiple output events into one summary

## Persistence
Consider optional file-based persistence for tracking across ntm invocations. Without persistence, delta tracking only works within single long-running session.

## Memory Bound
- Default max 1000 changes or 5 minutes of history
- Configurable via config file","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-10T02:53:57.473297-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T03:20:36.650095-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-10T03:20:36.650095-05:00","comments":[{"id":12,"issue_id":"ntm-hat","author":"jemanuel","text":"## Ring Buffer Design

```go
type StateChange struct {
```

### Coalescing Rules
Multiple rapid events should be coalesced:

```go
}
```

 {"id":"ntm-hlek","title":"EPIC: Output Deduplication & Comparison","description":"# Output Deduplication & Comparison

## Background & Motivation

When broadcasting the same prompt to multiple agents, users need to understand:
- Did agents produce similar or different results?
- Which files did each agent modify?
- Are there conflicts (same file, different changes)?

## CRITICAL REFRAME: Focus on FILE Changes, Not Text

Original approach: Compare scrollback text with similarity algorithms.
**Problem**: Scrollback is noisy (tool calls, errors, chatter). Comparing it is not useful.

**Better approach**: Compare WHAT AGENTS PRODUCED (file changes).

## What This Feature Actually Provides

### 1. File Modification Tracking
Track which files each agent modified:
```bash
ntm changes myproject
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ File Changes by Agent                                       │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ src/api/users.py                                            │
│   Modified by: cc_1, cc_2 (potential conflict!)             │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ src/api/auth.py                                             │
│   Modified by: cc_1 only                                    │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ tests/test_users.py                                         │
│   Modified by: cod_1, cod_2                                 │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### 2. Conflict Detection
```bash
ntm conflicts myproject
⚠️ Conflict detected: src/api/users.py
  cc_1: +25 -10 lines (added caching)
  cc_2: +18 -8 lines (added validation)
  
  View diff: ntm diff myproject cc_1 cc_2 src/api/users.py
```

### 3. File-Level Diff
```bash
ntm diff myproject cc_1 cc_2
# Shows git diff between uncommitted changes from each agent
# Uses git stash or worktrees if available
```

### 4. Unique Contributions
```bash
ntm contributions myproject
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Unique Contributions                                        │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ cc_1: src/api/auth.py (only agent to touch this)           │
│ cod_1: tests/integration/* (only agent on tests)           │
│ cod_2: docs/api.md (only agent on docs)                    │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## Implementation Approach

### File Change Detection
Option A: Parse git status per pane (if each pane has isolated changes)
Option B: Track file mtimes before/after prompts
Option C: Use git stash snapshots per agent

**Recommended**: Option A/B hybrid
- Snapshot file mtimes before broadcast
- After agents settle, compare to detect changes
- Attribute changes to agents based on timing

### Conflict Detection
```go
type FileChange struct {
    Path      string
    Agent     string
    Timestamp time.Time
    LinesAdded int
    LinesRemoved int
}

func DetectConflicts(changes []FileChange) []Conflict {
    byPath := groupByPath(changes)
    var conflicts []Conflict
    for path, agents := range byPath {
        if len(agents) > 1 {
            conflicts = append(conflicts, Conflict{Path: path, Agents: agents})
        }
    }
    return conflicts
}
```

## What We're NOT Doing

- NOT comparing scrollback text similarity (too noisy)
- NOT semantic analysis of code (too complex)
- NOT LLM-based summarization (expensive, unreliable)

Focus on concrete, observable file changes.

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Track file modifications per agent
- [ ] Detect same-file conflicts across agents
- [ ] ntm changes shows file attribution
- [ ] ntm conflicts highlights issues
- [ ] ntm diff shows file-level differences
- [ ] Works with git-based workflows","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:27:32.763201-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T17:34:27.468834-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-10T17:34:27.468834-05:00"}
 {"id":"ntm-hzl","title":"Define persona configuration schema","description":"# Define Persona Configuration Schema

## Purpose

Create the TOML schema for defining agent personas.

## Schema Definition

```toml
# ~/.config/ntm/personas.toml (or in config.toml)

[[personas]]
name = "architect"
description = "Senior software architect focused on system design"
agent_type = "claude"      # cc, cod, gmi
model = "opus"             # Model alias or full name
system_prompt = """
You are a senior software architect...
"""
temperature = 0.7          # Optional, agent-specific
context_files = [          # Optional, files to include in context
    "ARCHITECTURE.md",
    "docs/design/*.md"
]
tags = ["design", "review"]  # For filtering/discovery

[[personas]]
name = "implementer"
description = "Fast implementation engineer"
agent_type = "claude"
model = "sonnet"
system_prompt = """
You are an implementation engineer...
"""
temperature = 0.3
```

## Go Types

```go
type Persona struct {
    Name          string   `toml:"name"`
    Description   string   `toml:"description"`
    AgentType     string   `toml:"agent_type"`
    Model         string   `toml:"model"`
    SystemPrompt  string   `toml:"system_prompt"`
    Temperature   *float64 `toml:"temperature,omitempty"`
    ContextFiles  []string `toml:"context_files,omitempty"`
    Tags          []string `toml:"tags,omitempty"`
}

type PersonasConfig struct {
    Personas []Persona `toml:"personas"`
}
```

## Loading Strategy

1. Load built-in personas (compiled in)
2. Load user personas from ~/.config/ntm/personas.toml
3. Load project personas from .ntm/personas.toml
4. Later definitions override earlier (project > user > builtin)

## Built-in Personas

Include 5 default personas:
- architect
- implementer
- reviewer
- tester
- documenter

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] TOML schema defined and documented
- [ ] Go types with proper validation
- [ ] Multi-source loading with override semantics
- [ ] Built-in personas compiled in
- [ ] ntm config init includes persona examples","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:23:45.54837-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T04:32:58.545336-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-10T04:32:58.545336-05:00"}

 {"id":"ntm-ign","title":"Implement --robot-tail for multi-pane output streaming","description":"Tail recent output from all panes in a session, structured for AI consumption.

## Rationale
Knowing agents EXIST isn't enough - I need to see what they're DOING. Current approach requires separate tmux capture-pane calls per pane, each returning raw terminal output. This consolidates into one structured query.

## Usage
```bash
ntm --robot-tail myproject --lines=20
ntm --robot-tail myproject --panes=0.1,0.2 --lines=50
```

## Output Structure
```json
{

    "0.1": {
      "type": "claude",
      "state": "active",

      "truncated": false
    },
    "0.2": {
      "type": "codex",  
      "state": "idle",
      "lines": [...],
      "truncated": true
    }
  }
}
```

## Design Decisions
- Lines are raw strings (preserve formatting for context)
- Include agent type/state metadata inline (no second query needed)
- truncated=true means more output exists beyond --lines limit
- Empty panes return empty lines array (not omitted)
- Filter out ANSI codes but preserve newlines

 {"id":"ntm-iws","title":"Implement --persona flag for spawn/add","description":"# Implement --persona Flag for spawn/add

## Purpose

Allow spawning agents using persona definitions.

## Syntax

```bash
ntm spawn myproject --persona=architect --persona=implementer:2
ntm add myproject --persona=reviewer
```

## Flag Parsing

```go
type PersonaSpec struct {
    Name  string
    Count int  // Default 1
}

func ParsePersonaSpec(value string) PersonaSpec {
    parts := strings.SplitN(value, ":", 2)
    spec := PersonaSpec{Name: parts[0], Count: 1}
    if len(parts) > 1 {
        spec.Count, _ = strconv.Atoi(parts[1])
    }
    return spec
}
```

## Persona Resolution

```go
func ResolvePersona(name string) (*Persona, error) {
    // 1. Check project personas (.ntm/personas.toml)
    // 2. Check user personas (~/.config/ntm/personas.toml)
    // 3. Check built-in personas
    // 4. Return error if not found
}
```

## Spawn Integration

When spawning with --persona:
1. Resolve persona definition
2. Map agent_type to spawn flag (--cc, --cod, --gmi)
3. Set model from persona
4. Prepare system prompt for injection
5. Create pane with persona name in title

## Pane Naming with Persona

```
myproject__cc_architect_1
myproject__cc_implementer_1
myproject__cc_implementer_2
```

## Compatibility with Other Flags

```bash
# Persona + extra count
```

## Command Interface
```bash
ntm grep 'pattern' myproject          # Search all panes
ntm grep 'error' myproject --cc       # Only Claude panes
ntm grep 'def.*auth' myproject -i     # Case insensitive
ntm grep 'TODO' myproject -C 3        # 3 lines context
ntm grep 'pattern' myproject -n 100   # Search last 100 lines
ntm grep 'pattern' --all              # Search all sessions
```

## Output Format
```
myproject/cc_1:42: def authenticate(user, token):
myproject/cc_1:43:     if not validate(token):
myproject/cc_1:44:         raise AuthError("Invalid token")
--
myproject/cod_1:15: # TODO: Add authentication middleware
```

## Implementation
```go
func GrepPanes(session, pattern string, opts GrepOptions) ([]Match, error) {
    re, err := regexp.Compile(pattern)
    if err \!= nil {
        return nil, fmt.Errorf("invalid pattern: %w", err)
    }
    
    panes, err := tmux.GetPanes(session)
    if err \!= nil {
        return nil, err
    }
    
    var matches []Match
    for _, pane := range panes {
        if \!matchesFilter(pane, opts.Filter) {
            continue
        }
        
        output := tmux.CapturePaneOutput(pane.ID, opts.Lines)
        lines := strings.Split(output, "\n")
        
        for i, line := range lines {
            if re.MatchString(line) {
                matches = append(matches, Match{
                    Session: session,
                    Pane:    pane.Title,
                    Line:    i + 1,
                    Content: line,
                    Context: getContext(lines, i, opts.Context),
                })
            }
        }
    }
    
    return matches, nil
}
```

## Flags
| Flag | Description |
|------|-------------|
| -i | Case insensitive |
| -v | Invert match |
| -C N | Show N lines context |
| -A N | Show N lines after |
| -B N | Show N lines before |
| -n N | Search last N lines (default: all captured) |
| -l | List matching panes only |
| --cc, --cod, --gmi | Filter by agent type |
| --json | Output as JSON |

## Color Output
- Match highlighted in yellow/bold
- Pane name in blue
- Line numbers in grey
- Context separator (--) in dim

## Performance Considerations
- Capture is O(lines) per pane
- Pattern matching is O(chars)
- For large outputs, consider streaming
- Limit default capture to last 1000 lines

## Code Block Detection

### Markdown Fence Parsing
```go
type CodeBlock struct {
    Language  string   // From fence (python, bash, etc.)
    Content   string   // The code itself
    StartLine int      // Where in output
    FilePath  string   // Detected file path (if any)
    IsNew     bool     // Appears to be new file vs modification
}

func ParseCodeBlocks(output string) []CodeBlock {
    // Match 
    re := regexp.MustCompile("(?ms)^```(\\w*)\n(.*?)^```")
    matches := re.FindAllStringSubmatch(output, -1)
    
    var blocks []CodeBlock
    for _, m := range matches {
        block := CodeBlock{
            Language: m[1],
            Content:  m[2],
        }
        block.FilePath = detectFilePath(block.Content)
        blocks = append(blocks, block)
    }
    return blocks
}
```

### File Path Detection
Look for hints in the code or surrounding context:
```go
func detectFilePath(content string) string {
    // Check first line for path comment
    // # src/auth.py
    // // path/to/file.js
    // <!-- file.html -->
    
    // Check for module/package declarations
    // package main -> could be main.go
    // from fastapi import -> *.py
    
    return "" // If can't determine
}
```

## What This Task Does

Create a clipboard abstraction that works on macOS, Linux (X11/Wayland),
and WSL. This provides the foundation for clipboard-related features.

## Platform Detection

Detect platform and available clipboard tools:

macOS:
- pbcopy / pbpaste (always available)

Linux X11:
- xclip -selection clipboard
- xsel --clipboard

Linux Wayland:
- wl-copy / wl-paste

WSL:
- clip.exe (write only)
- powershell.exe Get-Clipboard (read)

Fallback:
- tmux's internal clipboard (with warning about limitations)

## Implementation

package clipboard

type Clipboard interface {
    Copy(content string) error
    Paste() (string, error)
    Available() bool
}

func New() (Clipboard, error) {
    switch runtime.GOOS {
    case "darwin":
        return &macOSClipboard{}, nil
    case "linux":
        if isWSL() {
            return &wslClipboard{}, nil
        }
        if isWayland() {
            return &waylandClipboard{}, nil
        }
        return &x11Clipboard{}, nil
    }
    return &tmuxClipboard{}, nil
}

## Error Handling

- Tool not found: Clear error with installation suggestion
- Permission denied: Suggest running with appropriate access
- Empty clipboard: Return empty string, not error

## Testing

Need to handle:
- CI environments (no display, no clipboard)
- SSH sessions (no local clipboard)
- Container environments

Skip clipboard tests or mock in these environments.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Clipboard works on macOS
- [ ] Clipboard works on Linux X11
- [ ] Clipboard works on Linux Wayland
- [ ] Clipboard works on WSL
- [ ] Graceful fallback when tools missing
- [ ] Clear error messages
